What We Monitor / Electrical Submersible Pumps (ESPs)

Asset type

Electrical submersible pump monitoring

ESPs operate 1,000–3,000 metres underground in extreme heat, high pressure, and corrosive fluids. When one fails, the only option is a full string pull: $150K–$500K per workover. After installation, there is zero physical access to the motor, pump, or cable. Every diagnostic insight must come from the surface or from downhole gauges that may not survive the run.

Talk to an engineer How ESA works on ESPs

Why ESPs are hard to monitor

No physical access. No traditional PdM. Every failure costs a workover.

After installation, an ESP is a sealed system. Accelerometers on bearings, oil analysis, ultrasound, manual inspection, none of the tools that work on surface equipment apply here. Operators are left with two data channels: surface electrical measurements and downhole gauges. When the gauges degrade, only the surface channel remains.

$150–500K

typical onshore workover cost: rig mobilisation, string pull, equipment replacement, and deferred production. Offshore: $3M–$5M.

1–5 years

typical ESP run life, depending on well conditions, fluid properties, and sand production. High-temperature and high-GOR wells see shorter runs.

50–61%

of ESP failures originate in the electrical chain in published field studies (motor, cable, penetrator). Range varies by field and conditions. These are the fault categories that surface ESA is designed to detect.

How ESA works on ESPs

Surface measurement through kilometres of cable

ESA uses the motor current and voltage, measured at the surface switchgear or VSD, as a sensor for the entire electromechanical chain. Load changes, mechanical faults, and electrical anomalies modulate the electromagnetic behaviour of the motor, producing detectable patterns in the current spectrum. For ESPs, this measurement happens through two physical filters that do not exist on surface-mounted assets.

Long power cable (1–3 km)

The downhole cable behaves as a distributed LC network. It attenuates high-frequency current harmonics needed for some ESA diagnostics and introduces reflections at cable-motor interfaces. Low-frequency content around the electrical fundamental, which carries most mechanical diagnostic information, survives this filtering. Higher-frequency fault signatures are progressively attenuated with cable length.

VSD switching noise

ESPs are almost always VSD-driven. PWM switching (typically 2–8 kHz) adds electrical noise to the current waveform. Fault signatures must be isolated from this noise through filtering and spectral analysis. In ESP applications, cable reflections from fast switching edges can produce motor-terminal overvoltages up to ~2× per-unit, increasing insulation stress.

What this means for detection capability

ESA on ESPs is not equivalent to ESA on surface-mounted motors. The long cable and VSD noise reduce signal-to-noise ratio, particularly for incipient faults and high-frequency signatures. Detection capability is conditional on cable length, VSD topology, operating point, and fault type.

ESA's strongest application on ESPs is detecting electrical faults in the motor and cable, the dominant failure category, from a surface measurement point that requires no downhole intervention and no sensor that can fail in the well.

What ESA detects on ESPs

Fault coverage from the surface: honest ratings

Detection capability on ESPs is conditional on cable length, VSD configuration, operating point, and fault type. Confidence ratings below reflect the physical constraints of surface measurement through long cables. Where downhole sensors provide stronger coverage, we say so.

Fault type What's happening How ESA detects it Confidence
Hard winding fault (phase-to-ground, phase-to-phase) Large asymmetry in motor windings from insulation failure, often triggered by thermal stress or voltage transients Clear current/voltage imbalance and negative sequence components at the surface drive. High signature contrast makes this detectable through cable attenuation. Full
Incipient insulation degradation Early-stage winding insulation breakdown from sustained high temperature, thermal cycling, or chemical exposure downhole Phase imbalance trends and impedance changes measured at the surface. Long cable attenuates early-stage signatures; VFD switching noise reduces signal-to-noise ratio. Conditional. Early-stage degradation may be undetectable on cable runs >2 km. One documented miss (CI-5570) where cable filtering and VFD masking combined to suppress the fault signature.
Rotor bar damage Cracked or broken bars in the induction motor rotor cage from thermal fatigue or high starting currents Characteristic sidebands at (1±2s)f around supply frequency, where s is motor slip. Classic MCSA domain. Well-understood physics. Full. Induction motors only. Not applicable to permanent magnet motors, which are increasingly adopted for ESP applications. Fault signatures differ fundamentally between motor types.
Supply voltage unbalance & power quality Voltage unbalance, harmonics, or undervoltage from the surface supply: a fault category downhole sensors cannot detect at all Direct measurement of voltage and current at the supply point. ESA is best-of-class for supply-side anomalies. Full
Sine wave filter degradation Asymmetric component degradation in the output filter, producing irregular harmonics and voltage imbalance post-VFD Even harmonics and phase-specific distortion patterns in surface current and voltage measurements. Confirmed in field detection (case study). Full
Power cable / MLE insulation breakdown Downhole cable insulation degradation, splice joint deterioration, or mechanical damage Phase imbalance and impedance change trends at the surface. Some faults manifest as unbalance/leakage/trips; fault location along the cable is not determinable from ESA alone. Conditional. Requires trending over time. Downhole insulation resistance measurement provides more direct cable health data where gauge is functional.
Gas interference / gas locking Free gas entering pump intake causing load oscillation, cavitation, or complete gas lock Load oscillation patterns and current surging visible at surface. Distinguishing gas effects from other instabilities (e.g., off-design operation, viscosity change) requires pressure/flow context. Conditional. Detects the effect (load change) but not always the root cause. Downhole intake pressure provides stronger, more direct detection.
Pump stage wear / erosion Gradual hydraulic efficiency loss from sand erosion, radial wear, or scale buildup on impellers and diffusers Progressive power/torque change at equivalent operating conditions. Detected via long-term trending, not direct spectral signature. Multiple confounders (GOR, viscosity, scaling). Conditional. Late detection. Downhole pressure differential provides direct, earlier measurement of pump head loss. ESA sees the electrical consequence, not the hydraulic cause.
Bearing wear / eccentricity Downhole motor bearing and pump bearing degradation from abrasive fluids or misalignment Torque ripple and airgap variation signatures in surface current. Attribution to specific bearing location is limited compared to direct downhole vibration measurement. Conditional. Detects "something mechanical is changing." Localisation is limited. Signal attenuated by cable length.
Protector / seal leakage Seal barrier failure allowing well fluid into motor oil cavity Indirect only: increased motor drag and thermal effects may eventually change current signature. No direct electrical indication of seal condition. Weak. Low specificity. Downhole motor temperature is a better indirect indicator.
Sand erosion (early-to-mid stage) Gradual abrasive wear of pump stages before hydraulic performance collapses Not reliably detectable. Small friction changes are attenuated by the long cable. Current signature unchanged until flow restriction forces speed/current changes. Not detectable. Downhole pressure differential is the only reliable method for early detection.
Cable connector / penetrator failure Connector degradation at surface junction box or downhole motor terminal Out of scope for motor ESA. Connector faults are external infrastructure, not motor winding faults. May appear as intermittent anomalies. Not detectable. Requires visual inspection, IR thermography, or cable resistance testing. One documented miss (CI-3964) confirmed this boundary.

Detection capability depends on cable length, VSD topology, sampling rate, operating point, and well conditions. Confidence ratings reflect the physical constraints of surface measurement through long cables. See ESA detection constraints.

Detection boundaries

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. ESA on ESPs faces harder physical constraints than on surface-mounted assets. These are the known limitations.

Cable length limits detection. Cables under 500 m: good signal quality for electrical fault detection. 500 m–2 km: marginal, attenuated signatures requiring VFD filtering optimisation. Over 2 km: cable acts as a strong low-pass filter; early-stage faults may be masked.
VSD switching noise. PWM drives (2–8 kHz switching) obscure fault signatures. Soft-starters produce lower noise and better ESA performance. Line-start ESPs (rare) provide the cleanest signal.
Mechanical faults detected late or not at all. Sand erosion, scale buildup, and early-stage bearing wear do not produce reliable electrical signatures through long cables. ESA sees the electrical consequence of severe mechanical faults, not early mechanical wear.
Variable-speed operation. Constant-speed operation produces the most repeatable signatures. Variable-speed production modulation makes fault classification harder. Part-load operation reduces signal-to-noise ratio.
Emerging capability. ESA on ESPs is an emerging application. Our current ESP dataset is small. Too small for statistical confidence intervals. Detection performance will be validated as the monitored fleet grows. We will update this page as evidence matures.

Detection performance depends on well geometry, cable characteristics, and fault progression rate. See full detection transparency report →

Monitoring architecture

What ESA covers. What downhole sensors cover. Where they overlap.

ESPs are the only motor-driven asset with zero physical access after installation. Every diagnostic insight must come from either surface instruments or downhole gauges. Electrical failures account for 50–61% of all ESP failures in published field studies. Though the exact split varies by well conditions and operator. ESA addresses these from the surface. Downhole sensors see the hydraulic and reservoir conditions that ESA cannot. Neither technology alone covers the full failure population.

ESA leads
  • Stator winding insulation degradation
  • Turn-to-turn shorts and phase-to-ground faults
  • Rotor bar damage (induction motors)
  • VSD output quality and power supply faults
  • Sine wave filter degradation
  • Supply voltage unbalance

These are the fault categories that downhole sensors either cannot detect or detect only through secondary thermal effects. They represent the majority of ESP failures.

Both contribute
  • Gas locking: ESA sees load loss, downhole sees pressure collapse
  • Off-design operation: ESA sees power deviation, downhole locates pump operating point
  • Cavitation: ESA detects broadband noise increase, downhole confirms intake pressure below bubble point
  • Cable faults: ESA sees impedance change, downhole sees insulation resistance decline

Combined surface and downhole data provides both detection and root-cause discrimination. Neither alone gives the full picture.

Downhole sensors lead
  • Pump stage degradation (direct pressure differential measurement)
  • Gas slugging characterisation (intake pressure instability)
  • Bearing wear (downhole vibration, where installed)
  • Motor thermal protection (winding temperature)
  • Reservoir monitoring (absolute pressure and temperature)

These are hydraulic, mechanical, and reservoir conditions that manifest most clearly in direct physical measurements at the pump.

The gauge survival problem: monitoring degrades, ESA does not

Downhole gauges operate in extreme conditions. Published reliability data shows a persistent survival challenge. ESA hardware sits in a benign surface environment and outlasts every ESP installation. The exact degradation curve is field- and vendor-specific, but the direction is consistent.

At installation
100%

Full downhole gauge data plus surface ESA. Maximum diagnostic visibility across all fault types.

Year 1
~65%

In high-temperature wells (>120°C), one-year gauge reliability reported at ~65% in a Saudi Aramco study. Communication dropouts observed when ESP activates (EMI on monoconductor systems).

Year 3–4
Varies widely

Survival depends on temperature class, telemetry architecture, and installation quality. Sensor drift, cable faults, and electronics failure compound over time. No universal industry figure exists for this interval.

Year 5+
~69%

Five-year survival probability of ~69% reported in a Shell analysis of 952 permanent downhole gauge systems (1993–1998 data). When gauges fail, surface ESA becomes the remaining continuous diagnostic layer. Surface hardware: 100% operational.

Gauge survival data: Shell analysis of 952 permanent downhole gauge systems (SPE-57057). One-year data: Saudi Aramco high-temperature well study. Modern telemetry may perform better. These are the best publicly cited benchmarks. We report what the evidence supports, not universal claims.

The case for a surface-based electrical monitoring layer

ESP workovers cost $150K–$500K onshore and $3M–$5M offshore. Deferred production adds $5K–$100K per day. The question is not whether to monitor, but how to maintain diagnostic coverage when downhole gauges degrade. And how to cover the electrical failure chain that accounts for the majority of ESP failures.

ESA is the only monitoring method that survives the full ESP run life without downhole intervention. It addresses supply-side and motor/cable electrical faults from the surface, fault categories that downhole sensors either cannot detect or detect only through secondary thermal effects. Downhole sensors are irreplaceable for hydraulic diagnosis, gas interference, and reservoir monitoring where they function. Together, they cover the full failure population.

ESA on ESPs is an emerging capability. Detection is conditional on cable length, VSD configuration, and fault type. We position it as a complementary surface layer that reduces the risk of running blind, not as a replacement for downhole gauges where they survive.

ESP detections

Documented detections on ESPs

These are real cases with published detail. We show what we have rather than listing unlinked claims.

Electrical / Filter Oil & Gas

Broken sine wave filter on ESP drive

SAM4 detected irregular harmonic patterns and voltage imbalance: even harmonics and phase-specific distortion not seen on other ESPs. Layered troubleshooting isolated the fault to a degraded sine wave filter. Component replaced, power quality restored. Without detection, long-term motor damage was the likely outcome.

Read full case study →
Efficiency Water & Wastewater

Submersible pump efficiency optimisation at Sabesp

SAM4 combined electrical signature data with flow and head telemetry to reveal that a different pump combination reduced specific energy consumption. Six-month pilot identified 1,050 MWh annual savings, €46–52K cost reduction, and 450 tonnes CO₂ avoidable emissions across Sabesp's submersible fleet.

Read full case study →

Our ESP detection library is growing. As documented cases accumulate, we will add them here. We do not list detection stories without published evidence.

See all case studies →

Installation on ESPs

Under 60 minutes at the surface drive panel. Zero downhole access.

1

Open the motor control cabinet

SAM4 installs at the MCC, the same panel your electricians already access. No confined space entry, no scaffolding, no process shutdown.

2

Clip sensor onto motor supply cables

Current and voltage sensors clip directly onto the existing cabling. No wiring changes. No interruption to the asset's operation.

3

Connect and commission

The SAM4 gateway connects via cellular (4G/LTE). No dependency on your IT network. Monitoring starts immediately, with first diagnostic results within 48 hours.

Full installation guide → Security & network architecture →

Where ESPs are monitored

Industries using SAM4 on ESPs

Oil & Gas

Onshore and offshore ESP installations. Run life extension and workover planning on production wells.

Water & Wastewater

Submersible pumps in lift stations and deep wells. Energy efficiency optimisation and fault detection on inaccessible assets.
